narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam. This model assesses the likelihood of passing the bar on the first try based on academic records, test scores, and personal details like yours. For you specifically, it estimated a 52.9% probability of failing the bar exam, which tipped the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of admitting students who might struggle post-graduation. The strongest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, well below the typical 5th or 6th decile—meaning your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers, signaling potential challenges ahead. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, which is close to the average of 3.21 and helped nudge the prediction toward passing. Your family income in the upper-middle bracket also worked in your favor, as it aligns with profiles of students who often succeed, unlike lower brackets that can drag predictions down. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd, while low compared to the average 5th or 6th, had a milder negative pull since it was less influential overall. Meanwhile, your full-time student status—shared by over 90% of applicants—provided a small boost, as did your LSAT score of 27, though it fell short of the typical 36-37 range and didn't sway things much. In essence, your solid undergrad record and background offered some reassurance, but the sharp drop-off in your later school grades stood out as the biggest red flag when compared to successful bar takers. While this decision stings, especially given your full-time commitment and stable family support, it's not the end of the road. I encourage you to focus on strengthening your academic trajectory—perhaps by retaking courses or boosting test prep—to improve those decile rankings, then reapply with fresh evidence of growth. Many students in similar positions have turned things around by targeting these key areas, and with your profile's positives as a foundation, you're well-positioned to do the same. Keep pushing forward; your determination as a white male from an upper-middle background has carried you this far, and it will serve you well.
